title: Software Architecture
summary: Thinking, modularity, characteristics, and the dozen styles every architect picks between — a working playbook for system design.

chapters.title: Thinking in Architecture
chapters.summary: What architecture actually is, what an architect actually does, and the mindset that separates structural decisions from coding ones.

chapters.title: Modularity
chapters.summary: Coupling, cohesion, and the small set of metrics that turn "well-organised code" from a vibe into a measurable property.

chapters.title: Components
chapters.summary: The granularity question — what's a component, where do you split, and how do you decide whether a feature lives here or in the next module over.

chapters.title: Characteristics
chapters.summary: The "-ilities" — the qualities your architecture is supposed to deliver. Identifying which ones matter, measuring them, and protecting them under continuous change.

chapters.title: Architecture Styles
chapters.summary: A tour of the named shapes — what each is, what each is for, and the dimensions to compare them on before picking one.

chapters.title: Layered
chapters.summary: The n-tier workhorse. What "layered" actually means, why it's still the right answer for most teams, and the pitfalls that turn good layers into the dreaded big ball of mud.

chapters.title: Modular Monolith
chapters.summary: One deploy, clean internal boundaries. Most of the benefits of microservices — team independence, change locality, clear ownership — without the network in the middle.

chapters.title: Pipeline
chapters.summary: Data flowing through stages, each transforming what came before. Underrated style — perfect for ETL, batch jobs, content publishing, and any system whose value is "input → transformations → output."

chapters.title: Microkernel
chapters.summary: A small core with plug-ins for everything that varies. The right shape for IDEs, browsers, build tools, and any product that needs to be extensible by users.

chapters.title: Service-Based
chapters.summary: A handful of coarse-grained services talking to each other. The pragmatic middle between monolith and microservices — most teams that say "microservices" actually want this.

chapters.title: Event-Driven
chapters.summary: Components communicate by publishing and subscribing to events. The right shape for fan-out workflows, audit trails, and cross-team decoupling — and the wrong shape for almost everything else.

chapters.title: Space-Based
chapters.summary: An architecture for extreme scale. Replicate state into in-memory grids; ditch the database in the request path. Niche, demanding, and the right answer for systems that genuinely need it.

chapters.title: Microservices
chapters.summary: Many small services, each independently deployed, each owning its data. The most-discussed style of the 2010s — and the one most often adopted prematurely. What the architecture actually is, what it costs, when it earns its keep.

chapters.title: Choosing a Style
chapters.summary: A decision framework for picking among the styles. Domain, scale, team, characteristics — the dimensions that actually decide, in the order they should be considered.

chapters.title: Decisions and Diagrams
chapters.summary: Recording architectural decisions, communicating designs, and managing risk — the meta-craft of doing architecture, not just having it.

chapters.title: Teams and Practice
chapters.summary: The soft side of the role. Working with teams, negotiating, leading without authority, and the laws of architecture revisited as practice.
